Home/Unexplained Mysteries/D.B. Cooper Hijacking: Ransom, Parachute, and the Unsolved Man in the Suit

Unexplained Mysteries

D.B. Cooper Hijacking: Ransom, Parachute, and the Unsolved Man in the Suit

On November 24, 1971, a passenger using the name Dan Cooper turned a short flight from Portland to Seattle into a ransom exchange and a second flight into darkness. He left the aircraft somewhere before it reached Reno, but no witness saw the exact moment, and neither his identity nor his fate was ever established.

Updated July 19, 2026

Category
Unexplained Mysteries
Tags
Unsolved Case, D.B. Cooper, Hijacking, FBI, Aviation Mystery
Read time
9 min read
Story Type
Unsolved Mystery
Source Basis
FBI case history and later public-history summaries of the NORJAK investigation
Updated
July 19, 2026

Quick Answer

A man ticketed as Dan Cooper hijacked Northwest Orient Flight 305, claimed that he carried a bomb, and demanded $200,000 and four parachutes. After receiving them in Seattle, he released the passengers and ordered the aircraft back into the air. The crew later detected activity at the Boeing 727’s rear stairs and a sudden change in the aircraft, leading investigators to estimate that he jumped over southwestern Washington. He was gone when the plane landed in Reno. A small part of the ransom was found beside the Columbia River in 1980, but Cooper’s identity, landing place, and survival remain unconfirmed.

The Passenger Who Bought a Ticket to Seattle

On the afternoon of November 24, 1971, Portland International Airport was moving passengers toward Thanksgiving journeys. At the Northwest Orient counter, a man in a dark business suit paid cash for a one-way ticket to Seattle. The name he gave was Dan Cooper. Nothing in the transaction identified him as anything more than another traveler taking a short regional flight.

Flight 305 was a Boeing 727 scheduled to make the brief trip north to Seattle. Thirty-six passengers and six crew members were aboard. Cooper took a seat toward the rear, where the aircraft’s distinctive aft door and stairway were behind him. At that point the stairway was simply part of the airplane’s design, not yet the exit around which the entire case would turn.

The aircraft left Portland at about 2:50 p.m. Cooper ordered a bourbon and soda and paid for it. Around him, the cabin remained routine. Passengers had no reason to watch the man in the suit, and the crew had no reason to separate his movements from the ordinary work of departure and drink service.

Shortly after three, Cooper handed flight attendant Florence Schaffner a folded note. She initially treated it as the kind of personal message a passenger might offer a flight attendant and did not immediately read it. Cooper called her attention back to the paper and told her that she should look at it because he had a bomb.

Schaffner read the warning and sat beside him as directed. Cooper opened his attaché case far enough for her to see wires, a battery, and red cylinders that resembled sticks of explosives. The crew could not test the device in flight, and the case later disappeared with him, so its actual capability was never established. Its appearance was enough to make the threat immediate.

Cooper told Schaffner what he wanted: $200,000 in twenty-dollar bills, four parachutes, and a fuel truck ready when the airplane reached Seattle. The money had to be delivered without an attempt to seize him. The parachutes included main and reserve units, equipment that would allow him to leave the aircraft or force authorities to believe that he intended to take someone with him.

Schaffner carried the demands forward. The cockpit crew—Captain William Scott, First Officer William Rataczak, and Flight Engineer Harold Anderson—alerted air traffic control and Northwest Orient. Flight attendant Tina Mucklow became the main intermediary with Cooper, moving between the rear cabin and the telephone used to communicate with the cockpit. A routine flight was now split into two worlds: passengers who still believed they were experiencing a delay, and a crew quietly managing a bomb threat.

The Flight Circles While the Ground Responds

The aircraft circled the Seattle area as afternoon light faded. Passengers were told that a mechanical problem was delaying the landing and that fuel had to be burned. The explanation kept the cabin orderly. Cooper, sitting near the rear, could watch the attendants and continue passing instructions while most people around him remained unaware that they were hostages.

As the airplane remained overhead, money was assembled from a Seattle bank and its serial numbers were recorded. Parachutes came from more than one local source. The delivery included two main parachutes and two reserve parachutes. One reserve was a nonfunctional training unit that had been supplied during the hurried collection. There is no indication that Cooper recognized that problem when the equipment arrived.

By the time the exchange was ready, Flight 305 had been in the air for hours rather than the expected short hop. At about 5:39 p.m., it landed at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport and taxied to a remote position. The area around the aircraft was kept dark to reduce the hijacker’s view of armed personnel and vehicles.

The plane stopped with every passenger still aboard. A person approaching the aircraft could trigger the threat; a delay could make Cooper more impatient. Mucklow carried the money bag and parachutes into the cabin. The ransom was now physically inside the aircraft, transformed from a demand passed through the crew into a heavy bag of recorded currency in Cooper’s possession.

The delivery changed what Cooper was willing to allow. He released all thirty-six passengers, along with Schaffner and flight attendant Alice Hancock. People who had spent the afternoon believing that their flight was delayed left the airplane and entered an airport already organized around a federal crime. Their release ended the largest immediate danger, but it did not end the hijacking.

Cooper kept Mucklow and the three cockpit crew members aboard. He still had the attaché case, the ransom, and four parachutes. He also had an aircraft whose rear stairway could be lowered. The next stage would no longer involve a cabin full of unwitting passengers. It would place five people inside a plane configured for a slow, low flight into a wet November night.

The Exchange Ends and the Second Flight Begins

While the Boeing 727 was refueled, Cooper gave the crew conditions for departure. He wanted to be flown toward Mexico City. The airplane was to remain at roughly 10,000 feet, travel at about 170 knots, keep its landing gear down, and use partial flaps. The cabin was to remain unpressurized. Those conditions sharply reduced the aircraft’s range, requiring a fuel stop in Reno.

Cooper also wanted the aft stairs lowered for takeoff. The crew determined that the aircraft could not safely depart in the exact configuration he first requested. After discussion, he accepted takeoff with the stairs raised, retaining the ability to lower them once airborne. This was one of the few points at which the physical limits of the aircraft altered his plan.

At about 7:36 p.m., the plane took off from Seattle. It turned south on the route toward Reno, flying lower and slower than an ordinary jetliner. Rain and darkness covered the terrain below. Other aircraft tracked or followed the flight, but the weather and the 727’s configuration did not give observers a clear view of the rear stairway.

In the cabin, Cooper opened and examined the parachute equipment. The ransom had been delivered in a bank bag, not in a pack designed for a jump. He cut suspension line from one reserve parachute and used it to secure the money. The cut equipment and the training reserve would later remain aboard, showing part of his preparation but not the final arrangement he carried out of the plane.

Mucklow remained with him while he worked. She was the last person known to have seen him aboard. Cooper eventually directed her to go forward and join the pilots. She entered the cockpit and closed the door, leaving him alone in the passenger cabin with the money, his attaché case, and the parachutes he had selected.

The Rear Stairs Open Into the Night

The crew maintained the demanded flight conditions as the aircraft crossed southwestern Washington. Behind the locked cockpit door, the cabin was silent. The pilots communicated with Cooper through the interphone, but the exchanges became brief. When a warning indicated a problem with the aft stairs, the crew offered assistance. Cooper answered that he did not need it.

Around eight o’clock, a cockpit indicator showed that the aft stair system had been activated. The stairs could descend into the airflow beneath the tail. The open exit connected the empty rear cabin directly to darkness, rain, and the moving air below the aircraft. Yet the indicator could not show whether Cooper was still inside, standing on the stairs, or already gone.

The plane continued south. A little after 8:00 p.m., the crew felt a change that later became the central timing clue. The aircraft reacted with a pressure change, vibration, or upward movement of the tail consistent with weight leaving the aft stairs. The pilots had experienced the airplane’s response, not witnessed a jump. Investigators therefore treated the event as an estimated departure time rather than direct observation.

The crew did not open the cockpit door to inspect the cabin while the flight continued. If Cooper remained aboard with the claimed bomb, doing so could renew the immediate danger. They flew on toward Reno with the aft stairs down, still unable to confirm whether the hijacker had left.

Flight 305 reached Reno at about 10:15 p.m. Emergency personnel and law enforcement surrounded the aircraft. The crew landed safely despite the lowered stairs, stopped, and left the plane. Armed officers then entered and searched the cabin.

Cooper was not there. The money bag, his attaché case, trench coat, and two parachutes had disappeared with him. Two other parachute units remained, including the reserve from which cord had been cut. His black clip-on tie and the airline ticket connected to the name Dan Cooper were still aboard. The cabin showed preparation and departure, but not the outcome after he passed beyond the stairs.

The landing established the last confirmed boundary of the event. Cooper had been inside the airplane after Seattle and was absent at Reno. The aft stairs had been activated during the flight, and the aircraft had reacted in a way consistent with a jump. Everything beyond that boundary—where he came down, whether his parachute opened, whether he kept the money, and whether he survived—had to be investigated without a direct witness.

The Search Begins With an Estimated Landing Zone

The FBI had learned of the hijacking while it was underway and opened a case known as NORJAK, short for Northwest Hijacking. Once the aircraft was secure, agents processed it for whatever Cooper had left behind. They collected the tie, ticket, parachute equipment, and fingerprints and recorded the crew’s descriptions. Cigarette butts he had left were taken during the early investigation but were later lost, removing a potential source of modern forensic evidence.

The witnesses gave investigators a physical description from hours of close contact. Composite sketches turned the ordinary-looking man in a suit into the public face of the case. Those images could generate leads, but they could not transform an alias into a legal identity. A resemblance might bring a person under investigation; it could not establish that the person had occupied Cooper’s seat.

Search planners next tried to convert the crew’s reported aircraft movement into a place. They combined the flight path, approximate time, speed, winds, and possible parachute behavior. The resulting zone lay in southwestern Washington, often associated with the country around Ariel, Lake Merwin, and the Lewis River. It was an estimate built from several variables, not a point observed from the air.

The terrain made the uncertainty consequential. Forest, water, farms, and rough ground offered many places in which a parachute, money bag, or injured person could be missed. Searchers examined areas on foot and from the air. The timing also worked against them: winter weather and the lack of a witnessed descent meant that the search began across a broad landscape rather than along a single track.

Teams found no body, main parachute, briefcase, or ransom bag. They did not find clothing or personal effects that could be tied to the man on the aircraft. The absence did not prove that Cooper had walked away, because a failed jump in that terrain might also leave little quickly visible evidence. It did not prove death, because a surviving hijacker had every reason to remove himself and his equipment.

The investigation therefore expanded beyond the ground search. The serial numbers of all 10,000 ransom bills were distributed so that recovered or spent currency could be recognized. Agents followed tips across the country, checked people with parachuting or aviation backgrounds, and compared candidates with the eyewitness descriptions and known timeline.

By the fifth anniversary, more than 800 suspects had been considered. Many could be eliminated by age, appearance, location, or other facts. Some attracted attention because they resembled the sketches; others because they had technical experience or later committed similar crimes. Each candidate created a new identification test, but none produced the combination of reliable physical evidence, witness agreement, and documented opportunity needed to close the case.

The name itself added confusion. The man had bought his ticket as Dan Cooper. “D.B. Cooper” arose from an early press error involving another person who was quickly cleared, but the mistaken initials became permanent in public memory. Investigators were searching for the individual behind an alias while much of the country learned a second name that the hijacker had not used.

The Evidence Narrows Questions Without Answering Them

The objects recovered from Flight 305 preserved parts of Cooper’s activity. The ticket confirmed the alias and journey. The tie showed what he had worn and later provided trace material and a partial DNA profile. The cut parachute cord showed how he had improvised with the equipment. None of those objects contained a verified identity.

The parachutes created another incomplete chain. Investigators knew which units had been delivered and which had remained in Reno. They could infer which main parachute Cooper took, but that parachute was never conclusively recovered. Without it, they could not inspect its deployment, determine where it landed, or connect material on it to a suspect.

The ransom offered a different possibility because every bill had a recorded serial number. If Cooper spent the money through ordinary channels, a bill might be recognized. Years passed without a confirmed circulation hit. That silence did not establish that all of the currency had been destroyed or hidden, since serial-number checking depended on someone noticing and reporting a match.

On February 10, 1980, eight-year-old Brian Ingram was clearing a place for a campfire with his family at Tena Bar, a sandy bank of the Columbia River. He uncovered deteriorated packets of twenty-dollar bills. The serial numbers matched Cooper’s ransom. The surviving fragments totaled $5,800.

The find was the first confirmed physical evidence from the ransom after the night of the hijacking. It proved that part of the money had reached the riverbank. It did not show whether Cooper had carried it there, lost it during descent, placed it somewhere from which water later moved it, or never controlled those packets after leaving the aircraft.

Investigators searched around the discovery site and studied the condition and arrangement of the bills. No body, parachute, or additional cache emerged. Tena Bar was not a simple confirmation of the original estimated landing zone, so the money complicated the geography rather than fixing it. A genuine piece of the crime had appeared miles and years away from the event, with the path between them missing.

The remaining ransom was not conclusively found. Cooper’s equipment was not recovered in a form that solved the jump. The tie’s later forensic testing did not yield a unique match to a named individual. Each important item established an action or connection, then stopped short of the next fact investigators needed.

A Long Investigation Reaches an Unsolved Ending

New names continued to enter the case over the following decades. Some came from relatives who believed a secret or confession fit the hijacking. Some came from similarities to later parachute crimes. Others were promoted through private investigations. The FBI compared leads with the record where possible, but resemblance, aviation experience, or suspicious behavior did not by itself identify the passenger in seat 18C.

Richard Floyd McCoy Jr. drew early attention after carrying out a similar parachute hijacking in 1972. He was arrested for that crime. The resemblance in method made comparison unavoidable, yet the FBI concluded that he did not match the consistent descriptions given by two flight attendants and ruled him out for additional reasons. A later crime that showed the jump was physically possible did not prove that the same man had made both jumps.

Other candidates remained subjects of public argument without producing a decisive evidentiary match. Investigators could test some claims against timelines, photographs, employment, fingerprints, or available DNA. In other cases, time had removed records or people needed for comparison. The case grew larger through tips while its central physical record remained almost unchanged.

Survival remained part of the same uncertainty. Cooper faced darkness, rain, cold, a non-steerable parachute, unsuitable clothing, an improvised money load, and a landing zone he could not precisely choose. Those conditions made the jump dangerous. The failure to recover a body or parachute left open the possibility that he survived and concealed his return, but it did not demonstrate that he succeeded.

The Tena Bar money could be made to fit more than one broad ending, but it completed none of them. If Cooper died, the packets might have separated from him or moved later. If he survived, he might have lost some money or abandoned it. The discovery proved possession by the hijacker before takeoff and presence at the riverbank in 1980; the events between those points were not recovered.

On July 12, 2016, after nearly forty-five years, the FBI announced that it was redirecting resources assigned to NORJAK to other priorities. The bureau retained the evidence and left open a narrow path for new submissions involving specific physical material, such as parachute components or ransom money. It did not announce that Cooper had been identified or that the jump had been reconstructed.

The case ended its active federal phase with the same final separation that began over Washington. The crew landed safely and could describe the man they had known for several hours. The aircraft preserved objects he had handled. A riverbank returned a small part of the ransom. But the person who entered the rear cabin as Dan Cooper passed out of the confirmed record when the stairs opened, leaving no verified identity or final state behind.

Evidence and the Investigation

The airline ticket establishes that the hijacker used the name Dan Cooper and paid cash for the Portland-to-Seattle flight. It does not connect that name to a birth record, employment history, or confirmed address. The later “D.B.” label belongs to the public history of the case, not to the ticket he purchased.

Cooper’s black clip-on tie remained on the aircraft. It supplied material for later forensic testing, including a partial DNA profile, but contamination, incomplete profiling, and the absence of a verified comparison prevented a unique identification. The cigarette butts collected in 1971 were lost, closing off another potential DNA path.

The parachute evidence shows that Cooper examined the delivered equipment, cut line from one reserve, and departed with two units. The non-steerable main parachute and improvised ransom load support the conclusion that the jump was hazardous. Because the parachute he used was never confirmed as recovered, the equipment cannot show whether it deployed normally or where it came down.

The aft stair indicator and the crew’s report of an aircraft movement provide the basis for the estimated jump time. They support the conclusion that Cooper left during the southbound flight, but no instrument recorded his body leaving and no person watched him jump. Flight path, wind, and timing uncertainty therefore widened the possible landing area.

The $5,800 found at Tena Bar is unquestionably linked by serial number to the ransom. Its value lies in that direct connection. Its limit is equally important: the packets do not carry a recoverable account of when they reached the Columbia River bank or whether their movement involved Cooper, another person, water, or earthmoving activity.

Suspects and Leading Explanations

Richard Floyd McCoy Jr. became a leading comparison because he committed a similar hijacking and parachute escape less than five months later. Investigators nevertheless found significant problems, including conflict with flight-attendant descriptions, and the FBI ruled him out. Similar method is evidence for comparison, not identity.

Kenneth Christiansen attracted attention because he worked for Northwest, had military parachute experience, and became the subject of circumstantial claims by people around him. His physical characteristics and the absence of a forensic link prevented those circumstances from becoming proof.

Robert Rackstraw had military aviation and parachuting experience and was promoted years later by a private investigative team. The FBI had examined him much earlier and did not establish a connection sufficient to identify him as Cooper. Public confidence in a theory did not add the missing physical match.

The two broad outcome explanations remain survival and death during or after the jump. The weather, equipment, clothing, darkness, and uncertain terrain support a high-risk assessment. The missing body and parachute leave room for survival. Neither side has the physical evidence required to move from possibility to conclusion.

Q&A

Was D.B. Cooper his real name?

There is no evidence that it was. The ticket said Dan Cooper. The initials “D.B.” spread through an early news-reporting error and became the name by which the case is known.

Did anyone see Cooper jump?

No. Tina Mucklow had gone to the cockpit, and the crew relied on the aft-stair indicator and a later movement of the aircraft. Those observations support an estimated jump time but not a witnessed departure.

Was any of the ransom money found?

Yes. In 1980, Brian Ingram found $5,800 in deteriorated twenty-dollar bills at Tena Bar on the Columbia River. Their serial numbers matched the ransom.

Is the case officially solved?

No. The FBI redirected active resources in 2016 but did not identify Cooper or determine his fate. It retained the case evidence and invited submission of specific new physical evidence.

Could Cooper have survived the jump?

He could have, but survival has not been proved. The jump conditions were dangerous, and no verified post-hijacking trace of Cooper has established either escape or death.

Story & Source Note

This draft prioritizes the FBI’s public case history and released files, then checks the event sequence against regional historical records and independent aviation or parachuting accounts. Approximate times and estimated locations remain labeled as such. Suspect claims and survival theories are separated from the continuous incident body because none has reached a verified identification.

  • Smithsonian Magazine — A Brief History of Airplane Hijackings, From the Cold War to D.B. Cooper
